items.q: How do you say 'I am going to Spain' in French?
items.why: Students confuse aller (to go - movement away from speaker) and venir (to come - movement towards speaker). 'Je vais' = I'm going; 'je viens' = I'm coming. Also: en + feminine country, au + masculine, aux + plural.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: Which means 'I go to the mountains'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Je vais à la montagne. “Je vais à la mer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Je passe les vacances en France
items.misconception: Je passe la vacances en France
items.random_wrong: Je passe des vacance à France
items.why: Correct answer: Je passe les vacances en France. “Je passe la vacances en France” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Read: 'Je passe deux semaines à la mer et une semaine à la montagne'. How many days total (1 week = 7 days)?
items.why: The real figure is 21. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
